content:
Hi!
I started a branch to change init! and pin_init! to use : instead of <-. This has the advantage of being a more rusty syntax, which may be easier for beginners. It also makes it understandable by rustfmt which is now able to format this code.
On the other hand, it may feel a bit more confusing because more magical.

I couldn't find a reason why this was done with `<-̀ in the first place, so please tell me if changing that was a bad idea.

I pushed a branch here if you want to have a look: https://github.com/a1phyr/linux/tree/improve_init

(Also this makes it possible to change the error type of Init and PinInit traits from a generic to an associated type, which feels more correct but may not be a net positive, see https://github.com/a1phyr/linux/tree/init_assoc_error_type)

Alphyr said:

I started a branch to change init! and pin_init! to use : instead of <-. This has the advantage of being a more rusty syntax, which may be easier for beginners. It also makes it understandable by rustfmt which is now able to format this code.

<- is used for in-place initialization while : is used for regular initialization.
It is in this paragraph.
Sorry if it was unclear: this is a proposal to use : for both in-place and regular initialization
How does the macro differentiate then?
It doesn't and makes regular initialization go through impl<T> Init<T> for T
Seems reasonable to me. You might want to sync with @Benno Lossin since he is doing a major overhaul of pin-init these days.

Alphyr said:

Hi!
I started a branch to change init! and pin_init! to use : instead of <-. This has the advantage of being a more rusty syntax, which may be easier for beginners. It also makes it understandable by rustfmt which is now able to format this code.
On the other hand, it may feel a bit more confusing because more magical.

I see, those seem like good advantages.

I couldn't find a reason why this was done with `<-̀ in the first place, so please tell me if changing that was a bad idea.

The reason is twofold, first we didn't have the impl<T> Init<T> for T from the get-go and secondly, I thought it might be good to be able to differentiate when code is run/vs just memory written.

I pushed a branch here if you want to have a look: https://github.com/a1phyr/linux/tree/improve_init

Thanks! As Andreas Hindborg already said, I'm currently working on synchronizing the kernel version with the userspace version (https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/pinned-init). And when that's done, I plan to convert both versions simultaneously to use syn, since the declarative macro is a lot of pain. (we will have syn in the kernel eventually, so while there will be a transition period with both syn and non-syn code existing simultaneously, eventually we will only have syn).
I don't want to introduce big changes to the declarative macro anymore, and I'd rather do this change with the syn version. I'll also have to think about if we want to have this change.

(Also this makes it possible to change the error type of Init and PinInit traits from a generic to an associated type, which feels more correct but may not be a net positive, see https://github.com/a1phyr/linux/tree/init_assoc_error_type)

Hmm that makes specifying the error type a bit annoying... While it does make more sense, it doesn't really feel better for a user and we also loose the ability to have a default. That is rather important, since the most used case is the Infallible error type.

so far the advantages/disadvantages of just using : are:

  • rustfmt works
  • newcomers less confused about the <- syntax
    • but this might also be a bad thing, they might overlook the macro and when they try to use a normal struct initializer, then it fails, confusing them
  • it is no longer possible to differentiate fields that are copied into vs have a custom initializer

Maybe I forgot some....

Overall I like the idea, since rustfmt not working is rather annoying
cc @Gary Guo

I don't want to introduce big changes to the declarative macro anymore, and I'd rather do this change with the syn version. I'll also have to think about if we want to have this change.

I don't think that it is a big change, as I only copied the code for <- to :, but I understand that you prefer waiting for things to synchronize.
I thought that we may not want to wait too long to make this kind of big changes though.

I'll also have to think about if we want to have this change.

OK, please let me know! Do you want me to open an issue on the userspace repo to discuss this?

Hmm that makes specifying the error type a bit annoying... While it does make more sense, it doesn't really feel better for a user and we also loose the ability to have a default. That is rather important, since the most used case is the Infallible error type.

Yeah, I agree with you, but I thought at least interesting to have a look at the possibility

Maybe I forgot some....

Some implicit conversions are no longer made by rustc (eg see devres.rs in https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/7db9b5f7edb5fe02ba095af2fdd748d3cda9d2b2
)

Benno Lossin said:

  • rustfmt works

This is a major one for me :sweat_smile:

Alphyr said:

I don't want to introduce big changes to the declarative macro anymore, and I'd rather do this change with the syn version. I'll also have to think about if we want to have this change.

I don't think that it is a big change, as I only copied the code for <- to :, but I understand that you prefer waiting for things to synchronize.

Yeah, I glanced at the changes, they aren't that big, but I have the syn changes planned since basically the beginning, so I'd much prefer if we did those first.

I thought that we may not want to wait too long to make this kind of big changes though.

We've had pin-init for quite some time, so having it a couple months later isn't a big deal. It's a bigger deal for the user-space version, since the kernel doesn't really care about "big changes" (even then it isn't that big anyways).

I'll also have to think about if we want to have this change.

OK, please let me know! Do you want me to open an issue on the userspace repo to discuss this?

Yeah sounds like a good idea.

Hmm that makes specifying the error type a bit annoying... While it does make more sense, it doesn't really feel better for a user and we also loose the ability to have a default. That is rather important, since the most used case is the Infallible error type.

Yeah, I agree with you, but I thought at least interesting to have a look at the possibility

Yeah, thanks a lot for trying this out!

Alphyr said:

Maybe I forgot some....

Some implicit conversions are no longer made by rustc (eg see devres.rs in https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/7db9b5f7edb5fe02ba095af2fdd748d3cda9d2b2
)

Oh right, that might also have been a reason for why we decided on ->, but @Gary Guo will probably remember better than me.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

  • rustfmt works

This is a major one for me :sweat_smile:

yeah for me too...
but coercions are also pretty important, so if some common one fails there, I don't think it's worth it.

I thought that we may not want to wait too long to make this kind of big changes though.

Yeah, the sooner, the better -- but I wouldn't worry too much, especially since we could have a transition period.

Having said that, different syntax for different things usually means less ambiguity (not just for humans, but tooling too, e.g. catching mistakes). The "confusion for newcomers" I don't think is a big deal -- in fact, you could argue it is less confusing, in the sense that they will see the new syntax, wonder what is going on, and learn it. In a way, it can be more confusing to have the same syntax, in fact, since you may not realize the difference.

So I think the only concrete advantage is rustfmt. Is there any workaround for that?

Miguel Ojeda said:

So I think the only concrete advantage is rustfmt. Is there any workaround for that?

What I usually do is replace <- by :, let rustfmt do it's job and then reverse the replacement
I am assuming rustfmt wouldn't commit to give us something custom for us (which may be false), but perhaps there is a way to make rustfmt work with it?

Benno Lossin said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

So I think the only concrete advantage is rustfmt. Is there any workaround for that?

What I usually do is replace <- by :, let rustfmt do it's job and then reverse the replacement

Yea, thanks for that hint. It is a hassle I would rather live without. Support in rustfmtwould be great.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I am assuming rustfmt wouldn't commit to give us something custom for us (which may be false), but perhaps there is a way to make rustfmt work with it?

there are a lot of macros in the Rust ecosystem that would benefit from custom formatting, so I think it's very unlikely that they will give us something fro this

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

So I think the only concrete advantage is rustfmt. Is there any workaround for that?

What I usually do is replace <- by :, let rustfmt do it's job and then reverse the replacement

Yea, thanks for that hint. It is a hassle I would rather live without. Support in rustfmtwould be great.

we might be able to have a script that just does that

Benno Lossin said:

What I usually do is replace <- by :, let rustfmt do it's job and then reverse the replacement

+1, sounds bad, but not worse than the usual state of C where most places do not even use a formatter...

Could we automate that? i.e. a best-effort script that reads a file, changes only the text inside those macro calls, calls rustfmt, finds the calls again, and pastes those back into the original file, and then figures out the inverse replacement. Since rustfmt should only touch whitespace generally, it should be doable I think.

In fact, we could use the same script to manually implement the "format the doctests' examples" case, since it is still unstable, and that one would be a huge win.

Benno Lossin said:

we might be able to have a script that just does that

Exactly, you beat me to it :)

Miguel Ojeda said:

In fact, we could use the same script to manually implement the "format the doctests' examples" case, since it is still unstable, and that one would be a huge win.

I think that doctests are much much more complicated, they can have lines beginning with # and those probably shouldn't be formatted, then you have to remove the /// or //! in front and pass it to the formatter...

So it for sure is possible, but a lot more complex I think

Miguel Ojeda said:

Could we automate that? i.e. a best-effort script that reads a file, changes only the text inside those macro calls, calls rustfmt, finds the calls again, and pastes those back into the original file, and then figures out the inverse replacement. Since rustfmt should only touch whitespace generally, it should be doable I think.

Yeah it should be rather straight forward, just remember for all invocations of [try_][pin_]init! which fields use <- and which use :, replace, format and then reverse the replacement. Maybe even append a _ to the field name to account for the extra character that <- takes up.
We could have : #[pin] being the pin-init syntax which both stands out as being special while is still (unstably) a Rust syntax, and hopefully is recognised by rustfmt?
More verbose though
that looks horrible though...
but you're probably right in that it is recognized by rustfmt
Perhaps we can use that as a replacement in the rustfmt script and replace it back
So we don't have to remember field names
but then the line length is skewed
so code that with <- fits on a single line might not when using : #[pin]

The #[pin] attribute could also be on the whole field, like this

pin_init!(Structure {
    #[pin]
    field: initializer(),
})

#[pin] is not the good name though, because this is for initialization in-place, not necessarily for pinning

Benno Lossin said:

I think that doctests are much much more complicated, they can have lines beginning with # and those probably shouldn't be formatted, then you have to remove the /// or //! in front and pass it to the formatter...

So it for sure is possible, but a lot more complex I think

Yeah, definitely -- to be clear, I meant a best-effort script, i.e. if it wouldn't work in e.g. 50% of the cases, it could still be quite useful. So not something we pass all the time nor make it into rustfmtcheck.

Alphyr said:

The #[pin] attribute could also be on the whole field, like this

pin_init!(Structure {
    #[pin]
    field: initializer(),
})

#[pin] is not the good name though, because this is for initialization in-place, not necessarily for pinning

hmm yeah that also is an option.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Benno Lossin said:

I think that doctests are much much more complicated, they can have lines beginning with # and those probably shouldn't be formatted, then you have to remove the /// or //! in front and pass it to the formatter...

So it for sure is possible, but a lot more complex I think

Yeah, definitely -- to be clear, I meant a best-effort script, i.e. if it wouldn't work in e.g. 50% of the cases, it could still be quite useful. So not something we pass all the time nor make it into rustfmtcheck.

I see. For the pin-init case though I think we can make it work 100% of the time

(For context, the kernel's clang-format is also "best-effort", in the sense that people cannot really use it in CI, but it is still useful to select+format local chunks in your editor quickly or even full files if you disable it in some regions etc.)

Alphyr said:

The #[pin] attribute could also be on the whole field, like this

pin_init!(Structure {
    #[pin]
    field: initializer(),
})

#[pin] is not the good name though, because this is for initialization in-place, not necessarily for pinning

I like this approach.
